corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: A popular framework for unsupervised learning is that of representation learning [1, 2], whose goal is to use unlabelled data to learn a representation that exposes important semantic features as easily decodable factors.

context_window.current: A popular framework for unsupervised learning is that of representation learning [1, 2], whose goal is to use unlabelled data to learn a representation that exposes important semantic features as easily decodable factors.
context_window.next: A method that can learn such representations is likely to exist [2], and to be useful for many downstream tasks which include classification, regression, visualization, and policy learning in reinforcement learning.

top_candidates.sentence: The core idea of using mutual information to induce representation can be applied to other methods like VAE , which is a promising area of future work.

To evaluate whether the mutual information between latent codes c and generated images G(z, c) can be maximized efficiently with proposed method, we train InfoGAN on MNIST dataset with a uniform categorical distribution on latent codes c ∼ Cat(K = 10, p = 0.1).

Specifically, InfoGAN successfully disentangles writing styles from digit shapes on the MNIST dataset, pose from lighting of 3D rendered images, and background digits from the central digit on the SVHN dataset.
It also discovers visual concepts that include hair styles, presence/absence of eyeglasses, and emotions on the CelebA face dataset.

A popular framework for unsupervised learning is that of representation learning , whose goal is to use unlabelled data to learn a representation that exposes important semantic features as easily decodable factors.

For example, for a dataset of faces, a useful disentangled representation may allocate a separate set of dimensions for each of the following attributes: facial expression, eye color, hairstyle, presence or absence of eyeglasses, and the identity of the corresponding person.
Thus, to be useful, an unsupervised learning algorithm must in effect correctly guess the likely set of downstream classification tasks without being directly exposed to them.
In this paper, we present a simple modification to the generative adversarial network objective that encourages it to learn interpretable and meaningful representations.
We describe how maximizing mutual information results in interpretable representations and derive a simple and efficient algorithm for doing so.
Goodfellow et al. introduced the Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN), a framework for training deep generative models using a minimax game.

For instance, when generating images from the MNIST dataset, it would be ideal if the model automatically chose to allocate a discrete random variable to represent the numerical identity of the digit (0-9), and chose to have two additional continuous variables that represent the digit's angle and thickness of the digit's stroke.

In this paper, rather than using a single unstructured noise vector, we propose to decompose the input noise vector into two parts: (i) z, which is treated as source of incompressible noise; (ii) c, which we will call the latent code and will target the salient structured semantic features of the data distribution.

We now propose a method for discovering these latent factors in an unsupervised way: we provide the generator network with both the incompressible noise z and the latent code c, so the form of the generator becomes G(z, c).
To cope with the problem of trivial codes, we propose an information-theoretic regularization: there should be high mutual information between latent codes c and generator distribution G(z, c).
Another intriguing line of work consists of the ladder network , which has achieved spectacular results on a semi-supervised variant of the MNIST dataset.
Lake et al. have been able to learn representations using probabilistic inference over Bayesian programs, which achieved convincing one-shot learning results on the OMNI dataset.

To the best of our knowledge, the only other unsupervised method that learns disentangled representations is hossRBM , a higher-order extension of the spike-and-slab restricted Boltzmann machine that can disentangle emotion from identity on the Toronto Face Dataset .
This paper introduces a representation learning algorithm called Information Maximizing Generative Adversarial Networks (InfoGAN).
Therefore, we propose to solve the following information-regularized minimax game:
